[BEST EVIDENCE CHUNK]: Ken Ohara’s One, published in 1970, presents a radical exploration of identity and human universality through a series of extreme close-up portraits of New Yorkers. Created while Ohara was in his late twenties and working as an assistant to Hiro and Richard Avedon, the series reflects a deep engagement with the visual language of portraiture while challenging conventional notions of individual recognition. The project's visual approach systematically removes distinguishing features such as hair, clothing, and even racial characteristics by unifying the tonal range of the prints, directing the viewer’s focus solely on the facial features—eyes, nose, and mouth—in a way that both emphasizes difference and underscores commonality.

[PUBLISHED DATE]: 2019
[TITLE]: Edelle Kenny, The Interview — HAUTE so FABULOUS
[METADATA/INTRO]: Edelle Kenny is an Irish, New York City based photographer, videographer and freelance TV producer. She has worked on major global campaigns, shooting top models and influencers, not to mention her work as a producer with the Discovery Channel. Edelle’s...
[BEST EVIDENCE CHUNK]: What’s your favourite photograph that you’ve taken.. and the one you wish you’d taken? 
 I go through phases but the one that immediately comes to mind is of a dear friend Ruthy. Ruth is a beautiful girl who modelled before and we see each other every week, on paper it seemed like the perfect match to shoot immediately but, we didn’t. Something didn’t feel right and I am glad we waited. When we met we were very chalk and cheese and as our friendship developed we began to understand each other more and right before she left New York I asked her to shoot. This photo is the perfect merge between our personalities. Sharp, blurred lines, sexual and reserved.

[PUBLISHED DATE]: 2011-03-10
[TITLE]: Photographer Interview: Marcia Michael | Dodge & Burn: Decolonizing Photography History
[METADATA/INTRO]: Photographer Interview: Marcia Michael A few months ago I came across UK photographer Marcia Michael‘s series of black and white portraits titled “Study of Kin” on FotoVisura. I was touched by her desire yet failure to find historical images of...
[BEST EVIDENCE CHUNK]: I love fashion too, especially Steven Meisel. 
 | Portrait of a Young Girl from the series “The Study of Kin”, Copyright Marcia Michael | 
 D&B: Have you experienced any setbacks or different treatment along your photography career that you would attribute to being a woman and/or photographer of color? (this question is optional) 
 MM: Everything takes longer. 
 D&B: When did you realize you could have a career in photography? Describe your journey towards becoming a working photographer. 
 MM: To be very honest, its when I realized that photography is all I wanted to do, then there was no other option.
